content:
# Luke Chapter 16 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 108-109 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_016_page_001.jpg, LUK_016_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And he said also to his disciples, "There was a certain rich man who had a steward, and this one was accused to him as squandering his possessions.

**2** And having called him, he said to him, 'What is this I hear concerning you? Render the account of your stewardship, for you are no longer able to be steward.'

**3** And the steward said within himself, 'What shall I do, since my lord is taking away the stewardship from me? I am not able to dig; I am ashamed to beg.

**4** I know what I shall do, so that when I am removed from the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.'

**5** And having summoned each one of his lord's debtors, he said to the first, 'How much do you owe my lord?'

**6** And he said, 'A hundred baths of oil.' And he said to him, 'Take your written bond, and sit down quickly, and write fifty.'

**7** Then to another he said, 'And you, how much do you owe?' And he said, 'A hundred cors of wheat.' He said to him, 'Take your written bond, and write eighty.'

**8** And the lord commended the steward of unrighteousness, because he had acted shrewdly; for the sons of this age are more shrewd than the sons of light in relation to their own generation.

**9** And I say to you, make for yourselves friends by means of the mammon of unrighteousness, so that when it fails, they may receive you into the eternal tents.

**10** He who is faithful in a very little thing is faithful also in much; and he who is unrighteous in a very little thing is unrighteous also in much.

**11** If therefore you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will entrust to you the true riches?

**12** And if you have not been faithful in that which is another's, who will give to you that which is your own?

**13** No household servant is able to serve two lords; for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will hold to one and despise the other. You are not able to serve God and mammon."

**14** And the Pharisees, being lovers of money, heard all these things, and they were scoffing at him.

**15** And he said to them, "You are those who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts; for that which is exalted among men is an abomination before God.

**16** The law and the prophets were until John; from that time the kingdom of God is proclaimed as good tidings, and everyone forces his way into it.

**17** But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one stroke of the law to fall.

**18** Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and he who marries one divorced from a husband commits adultery.

**19** Now there was a certain rich man, and he was clothed in purple and fine linen, feasting sumptuously every day.

**20** And a certain poor man named Lazarus had been laid at his gate, covered with sores,

**21** and longing to be fed from the things falling from the table of the rich man; but even the dogs, coming, were licking his sores.

**22** And it came to pass that the poor man died and was carried away by the angels to the bosom of Abraham. And the rich man also died and was buried.

**23** And in Hades, having lifted up his eyes, being in torments, he sees Abraham from afar and Lazarus in his bosom.

**24** And he, having cried out, said, 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am in anguish in this flame.'

**25** But Abraham said, 'Child, remember that you received your good things in your life, and Lazarus likewise the evil things; but now here he is comforted, and you are in anguish.

**26** And in addition to all these things, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, so that those wishing to cross over from here to you are not able, nor may those from there cross over to us.'

**27** And he said, 'I ask you then, father, that you send him to my father's house,

**28** for I have five brothers, so that he may testify to them, lest they also come to this place of torment.'

**29** But Abraham said, 'They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them.'

**30** And he said, 'No, father Abraham, but if someone from the dead should go to them, they will repent.'

**31** And he said to him, 'If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be persuaded if someone should rise from the dead.'"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **16:1 — "squandering his possessions" (diaskopizōn ta hyparchonta autou):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΑΣΚΟΡΠΙΖΩΝ ΤΑ ΥΠΑΡΧΟΝΤΑ ΑΥΤΟΥ. The participle ΔΙΑΣΚΟΡΠΙΖΩΝ ("scattering, squandering") is the same verb used of the prodigal son in 15:13 (ΔΙΕΣΚΟΡΠΙΣΕΝ), creating a deliberate verbal link between the two parables. The prodigal squandered his own inheritance; this steward squanders another's possessions. The verb ΔΙΕΒΛΗΘΗ ("was accused, was slandered") from ΔΙΑΒΑΛΛΩ — the root from which ΔΙΑΒΟΛΟΣ ("devil, slanderer") derives — leaves ambiguous whether the charge was true or merely alleged. The narrative proceeds as though the accusation is accepted without investigation.

2. **16:3 — "I am not able to dig; I am ashamed to beg" (skaptein ouk ischuō, epaitein aischynomai):** Sinaiticus reads ΣΚΑΠΤΕΙΝ ΟΥΚ ΙΣΧΥΩ ΕΠΑΙΤΕΙΝ ΑΙΣΧΥΝΟΜΑΙ. The steward's self-assessment is brutally honest: ΣΚΑΠΤΕΙΝ ("to dig") represents manual labor for which he lacks physical capacity (ΟΥΚ ΙΣΧΥΩ, "I have not the strength"), while ΕΠΑΙΤΕΙΝ ("to beg") represents public degradation from which he shrinks in shame (ΑΙΣΧΥΝΟΜΑΙ). The two infinitives frame the steward's social predicament — he is a man of ledgers, not limbs, and too proud for the street. The internal monologue (ΕΙΠΕΝ ΕΝ ΕΑΥΤΩ, "he said within himself") parallels the prodigal's self-reflection in 15:17.

3. **16:6 — "a hundred baths of oil" (hekaton batous elaiou):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΚΑΤΟΝ ΒΑΤΟΥΣ ΕΛΑΙΟΥ. The ΒΑΤΟΣ (Hebrew bath) was a liquid measure of approximately 22 liters (roughly 6 gallons). One hundred baths of olive oil would represent approximately 2,200 liters — a very substantial quantity worth roughly 1,000 denarii (nearly three years' wages for a laborer). The reduction to fifty represents a discount of approximately 500 denarii. The instruction ΔΕΞΑΙ ΣΟΥ ΤΑ ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΑ ("take your written bonds") refers to the written debt instruments — the steward has authority to alter the legal documents because he still holds the position, however briefly.

4. **16:7 — "a hundred cors of wheat" (hekaton korous sitou):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΚΑΤΟΝ ΚΟΡΟΥΣ ΣΙΤΟΥ. The ΚΟΡ (Hebrew kor) was a dry measure of approximately 220 liters (about 6.3 bushels). One hundred cors of wheat was an enormous quantity — roughly the yield of approximately 100 acres. The reduction from one hundred to eighty represents a 20% discount, smaller in percentage than the oil reduction (50%) but comparable in absolute monetary value, suggesting the steward calculated his fraud to produce roughly equal obligations from each debtor.

5. **16:8 — "the lord commended the steward of unrighteousness" (epēnesen ho kyrios ton oikonomon tēs adikias):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΠΗΝΕΣΕΝ Ο ΚΥΡΙΟΣ ΤΟΝ ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΟΝ ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ. The identity of Ο ΚΥΡΙΟΣ ("the lord") is debated: it may be the lord within the parable (the rich man) or Luke's designation of Jesus as Lord. The genitive ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ ("of unrighteousness") is a Semitic quality-genitive: "the unrighteous steward." The commendation is for shrewdness (ΟΤΙ ΦΡΟΝΙΜΩΣ ΕΠΟΙΗΣΕΝ, "because he acted shrewdly"), not for dishonesty. The phrase ΟΙ ΥΙΟΙ ΤΟΥ ΑΙΩΝΟΣ ΤΟΥΤΟΥ ("the sons of this age") employs the Semitic idiom where "son of" denotes characteristic — those characterized by this present age versus those characterized by light.

6. **16:9 — "the mammon of unrighteousness" (tou mamōna tēs adikias):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΥ ΜΑΜΩΝΑ ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ. ΜΑΜΩΝΑΣ is a transliteration of the Aramaic word mamona ("wealth, property, that in which one trusts"), here personified and qualified by ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ ("of unrighteousness"). The phrase ΤΑΣ ΑΙΩΝΙΟΥΣ ΣΚΗΝΑΣ ("the eternal tents") is striking: ΣΚΗΝΗ ("tent, tabernacle") echoes the wilderness tabernacle and the Feast of Tabernacles, yet here modified by ΑΙΩΝΙΟΥΣ ("eternal") — temporary shelters made permanent. The verb ΕΚΛΙΠΗ ("when it fails") uses the subjunctive of ΕΚΛΕΙΠΩ, from which English "eclipse" derives — when mammon fails, is eclipsed, gives out.

7. **16:10 — "faithful in a very little thing" (pistos en elachistō):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΙΣΤΟΣ ΕΝ ΕΛΑΧΙΣΤΩ. The superlative ΕΛΑΧΙΣΤΟΣ ("smallest, very least") forms a sharp contrast with ΠΟΛΛΩ ("much"). The four-part chiastic structure — faithful/little, faithful/much, unrighteous/little, unrighteous/much — establishes a universal principle of proportional character: behavior in small matters reveals and determines behavior in great matters. This logion connects the parable (management of another's wealth) to the larger theme (faithfulness with spiritual stewardship).

8. **16:13 — "No household servant is able to serve two lords" (oudeis oiketēs dynatai dysi kyriois douleuein):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΟΙΚΕΤΗΣ ΔΥΝΑΤΑΙ ΔΥΣΙ ΚΥΡΙΟΙΣ ΔΟΥΛΕΥΕΙΝ. Sinaiticus includes ΟΙΚΕΤΗΣ ("household servant"), which is absent from the parallel in Matthew 6:24. The word ΟΙΚΕΤΗΣ (from ΟΙΚΟΣ, "house") denotes a servant whose life is entirely within the master's household, making divided loyalty structurally impossible. The verb ΔΟΥΛΕΥΕΙΝ ("to serve as a slave") describes absolute allegiance, not mere employment. The final declaration ΟΥ ΔΥΝΑΣΘΕ ΘΕΩ ΔΟΥΛΕΥΕΙΝ ΚΑΙ ΜΑΜΩΝΑ ("you are not able to serve God and mammon") places God and mammon as rival masters — mammon is implicitly personified as a deity demanding total devotion.

9. **16:14 — "being lovers of money" (philargyroi hyparchontes):** Sinaiticus reads ΦΙΛΑΡΓΥΡΟΙ ΥΠΑΡΧΟΝΤΕΣ. The compound adjective ΦΙΛΑΡΓΥΡΟΣ (from ΦΙΛΟΣ + ΑΡΓΥΡΟΣ, "lover of silver") characterizes the Pharisees' fundamental disposition. The participle ΥΠΑΡΧΟΝΤΕΣ ("being by nature, existing as") suggests this is not an occasional vice but a constitutive quality. The verb ΕΞΕΜΥΚΤΗΡΙΖΟΝ ("they were turning up their noses at, sneering at") is vivid — from ΜΥΚΤΗΡ ("nose"), it literally means "they were wrinkling their noses" at Jesus. The imperfect tense indicates repeated, sustained mockery.

10. **16:15 — "an abomination before God" (bdelygma enōpion tou Theou):** Sinaiticus reads ΒΔΕΛΥΓΜΑ ΕΝΩΠΙΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ. The word ΒΔΕΛΥΓΜΑ ("abomination, detestable thing") is a term of extreme cultic revulsion, used in the LXX for idolatry and ritual pollution (cf. Dan 9:27; 11:31 — "the abomination of desolation"). To apply it to that which is ΥΨΗΛΟΝ ΕΝ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΙΣ ("exalted among men") creates a complete inversion of values: what human society prizes most, God finds most revolting. The contrast between ΕΝΩΠΙΟΝ ΤΩΝ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΩΝ ("before men") and ΕΝΩΠΙΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ("before God") structures the verse as a judgment on two audiences.

11. **16:16 — "everyone forces his way into it" (pas eis autēn biazetai):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΣ ΕΙΣ ΑΥΤΗΝ ΒΙΑΖΕΤΑΙ. The verb ΒΙΑΖΕΤΑΙ is either middle ("everyone forces his way in") or passive ("everyone is being forced into it"). The middle reading, preferred by most interpreters, conveys violent urgency — the kingdom is not entered casually but seized with determined effort. The phrase Ο ΝΟΜΟΣ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΠΡΟΦΗΤΑΙ ΜΕΧΡΙ ΙΩΑΝΝΟΥ ("the law and the prophets until John") establishes John the Baptist as the hinge of salvation history: before John, the law and prophets; from John onward, the kingdom is proclaimed. This does not abolish the law (v. 17) but marks a new era of its fulfillment.

12. **16:17 — "one stroke of the law" (mian keraian tou nomou):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΙΑΝ ΚΕΡΑΙΑΝ ΤΟΥ ΝΟΜΟΥ. The ΚΕΡΑΙΑ ("horn, projection, serif") refers to the tiny decorative strokes on Hebrew letters — the smallest distinguishing marks of the written Torah. The hyperbole is profound: heaven and earth will pass away more easily than the smallest calligraphic mark of the law will be annulled. This logion, placed between the proclamation of the kingdom (v. 16) and the teaching on divorce (v. 18), affirms that the new era of the kingdom does not render the law void but intensifies its claims.

13. **16:18 — "commits adultery" (moicheuei):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΟΙΧΕΥΕΙ. The present tense ΜΟΙΧΕΥΕΙ indicates a continuous state, not a single act — the remarriage constitutes an ongoing condition of adultery. The verse addresses two scenarios: the man who divorces and remarries, and the man who marries a divorced woman. Both are declared adulterous. This saying, appearing abruptly between teachings on the law (v. 17) and the parable of the rich man (v. 19), serves as a concrete illustration of verse 17: the law's demands on marriage remain binding in the era of the kingdom.

14. **16:19 — "purple and fine linen" (porphyran kai bysson):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΟΡΦΥΡΑΝ ΚΑΙ ΒΥΣΣΟΝ. ΠΟΡΦΥΡΑ ("purple") was the most expensive dye in the ancient world, extracted from the murex sea snail — a single garment could cost more than a laborer earned in a lifetime. ΒΥΣΣΟΣ ("fine linen, byssus") was imported Egyptian linen of extraordinary fineness. Together they denote extreme, conspicuous wealth. The participle ΕΥΦΡΑΙΝΟΜΕΝΟΣ ("feasting, making merry") uses the same verb applied to the celebration in 15:23-24, 32 — creating an ironic echo: the father's feast celebrated restoration, but the rich man's daily feast celebrates only himself. The adverb ΚΑΘ ΗΜΕΡΑΝ ΛΑΜΠΡΩΣ ("every day sumptuously") indicates that what should be extraordinary has become routine.

15. **16:20 — "a certain poor man named Lazarus" (ptōchos de tis onomati Lazaros):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΤΩΧΟΣ ΔΕ ΤΙΣ ΟΝΟΜΑΤΙ ΛΑΖΑΡΟΣ. This is the only character in any of Jesus's parables given a proper name. ΛΑΖΑΡΟΣ is the Greek form of the Hebrew ΕΛΕΑΖΑΡ (Eleazar, "God helps"). The irony is sharp: the man whose name means "God helps" appears utterly helpless. The verb ΕΒΕΒΛΗΤΟ ("had been laid, had been cast") is the pluperfect passive of ΒΑΛΛΩ — he was not positioned voluntarily but deposited, thrown down at the gate. The word ΠΥΛΩΝ ("gate") denotes not a simple door but an elaborate gateway, emphasizing the grandeur of the rich man's residence. ΕΙΛΚΩΜΕΝΟΣ ("covered with sores, ulcerated") is a perfect passive participle indicating a chronic condition.

16. **16:21 — "the things falling from the table" (tōn piptontōn apo tēs trapezēs):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΩΝ ΠΙΠΤΟΝΤΩΝ ΑΠΟ ΤΗΣ ΤΡΑΠΕΖΗΣ. The present participle ΠΙΠΤΟΝΤΩΝ ("falling") suggests crumbs and scraps that continually fall — Lazarus desires not a plate but the debris of excess. The phrase ΑΛΛΑ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΚΥΝΕΣ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΙ ΕΠΕΛΕΙΧΟΝ ΤΑ ΕΛΚΗ ΑΥΤΟΥ ("but even the dogs, coming, were licking his sores") deepens the degradation: the dogs are not pets but scavenging street dogs, ritually unclean animals. Their licking is not comfort but contamination — the imperfect ΕΠΕΛΕΙΧΟΝ indicates this was a repeated indignity. Lazarus is too weak to drive them away.

17. **16:22 — "the bosom of Abraham" (ton kolpon Abraam):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΝ ΚΟΛΠΟΝ ΑΒΡΑΑΜ. The ΚΟΛΠΟΣ ("bosom, chest, lap") denotes the position of honor at a banquet — reclining against the host's chest, as the beloved disciple reclined against Jesus (John 13:23). The image reverses Lazarus's earthly condition entirely: from lying at the gate to reclining at the patriarchal banquet. The rich man receives only ΕΤΑΦΗ ("he was buried") — his funeral is mentioned but his destination is Hades. The contrast is absolute: Lazarus is carried by angels; the rich man is carried to a tomb.

18. **16:23 — "in Hades" (en tō hadē):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΩ ΑΔΗ. ΑΔΗΣ (Hades) is the Greek rendering of the Hebrew SHEOL — the realm of the dead, here depicted as a place of conscious torment. The phrase ΥΠΑΡΧΩΝ ΕΝ ΒΑΣΑΝΟΙΣ ("being in torments") uses ΒΑΣΑΝΟΣ, originally the touchstone used to test gold by rubbing it against a dark stone — metaphorically, a test or trial, then torture. The rich man SEES (ΟΡΑ) Abraham and Lazarus ΑΠΟ ΜΑΚΡΟΘΕΝ ("from afar") — proximity enough to perceive but distance that cannot be bridged. The visual access intensifies the torment.

19. **16:24 — "Father Abraham" (pater Abraam):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΤΕΡ ΑΒΡΑΑΜ. The rich man addresses Abraham as "father," claiming covenantal kinship — he is, after all, a son of Abraham by birth. The request for Lazarus to DIP (ΒΑΨΗ) the TIP (ΑΚΡΟΝ) of his FINGER (ΔΑΚΤΥΛΟΥ) in WATER (ΥΔΑΤΟΣ) and COOL (ΚΑΤΑΨΥΞΗ) his TONGUE (ΓΛΩΣΣΑΝ) is pathetically minimal — the smallest possible relief. The verb ΚΑΤΑΨΥΧΩ ("to cool down") occurs only here in the New Testament. The phrase ΟΔΥΝΩΜΑΙ ΕΝ ΤΗ ΦΛΟΓΙ ΤΑΥΤΗ ("I am in anguish in this flame") uses ΟΔΥΝΑΩ ("to cause pain, to torment"), which also appeared in 2:48 where Mary said to the boy Jesus "we were searching for you in anguish." Note that the rich man still views Lazarus as a servant to be dispatched on errands — his social assumptions persist even in Hades.

20. **16:25 — "Child, remember" (teknon, mnēsthēti):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΕΚΝΟΝ ΜΝΗΣΘΗΤΙ. Abraham addresses the rich man as ΤΕΚΝΟΝ ("child") — the same tender address the father used for the elder son in 15:31. The imperative ΜΝΗΣΘΗΤΙ ("remember") calls for active recollection: ΑΠΕΛΑΒΕΣ ΤΑ ΑΓΑΘΑ ΣΟΥ ΕΝ ΤΗ ΖΩΗ ΣΟΥ ("you received your good things in your life"). The verb ΑΠΕΛΑΒΕΣ (from ΑΠΟΛΑΜΒΑΝΩ, "to receive in full, to receive what is owed") implies that the rich man received his complete portion — there is nothing further owed to him. The contrast ΣΥ/ΤΑ ΑΓΑΘΑ versus ΛΑΖΑΡΟΣ/ΤΑ ΚΑΚΑ ("you/good things" versus "Lazarus/evil things") is starkly parallel. The adverb ΝΥΝ ΔΕ ("but now") marks the eschatological reversal: ΩΔΕ ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΕΙΤΑΙ ΣΥ ΔΕ ΟΔΥΝΑΣΑΙ ("here he is comforted, but you are in anguish").

21. **16:26 — "a great chasm has been fixed" (chasma mega estēriktai):** Sinaiticus reads ΧΑΣΜΑ ΜΕΓΑ ΕΣΤΗΡΙΚΤΑΙ. The word ΧΑΣΜΑ ("chasm, yawning gap") occurs only here in the New Testament — from ΧΑΙΝΩ ("to gape open"), it denotes a vast, unbridgeable divide. The perfect passive ΕΣΤΗΡΙΚΤΑΙ ("has been fixed, has been established") from ΣΤΗΡΙΖΩ indicates that the chasm was established by divine decree and remains permanently in place. The purpose clause ΟΠΩΣ ΟΙ ΘΕΛΟΝΤΕΣ ΔΙΑΒΗΝΑΙ ... ΜΗ ΔΥΝΩΝΤΑΙ ("so that those wishing to cross over may not be able") makes the impossibility absolute — it is not merely difficult but ontologically impossible. This permanence answers the implied question: there is no post-mortem correction.

22. **16:27-28 — "send him to my father's house" (pempsēs auton eis ton oikon tou patros mou):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΕΜΨΗΣ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΟΙΚΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΠΑΤΡΟΣ ΜΟΥ. The rich man, having failed to secure relief for himself, now intercedes for his five brothers. The request reveals several things: he knows his brothers live as he did; he believes they face the same fate; and he still treats Lazarus as an errand-runner. The verb ΔΙΑΜΑΡΤΥΡΗΤΑΙ ("that he may testify, that he may solemnly warn") is an intensified form of ΜΑΡΤΥΡΕΩ — not mere information but urgent, solemn testimony. The phrase ΤΟΠΟΝ ΤΟΥΤΟΝ ΤΗΣ ΒΑΣΑΝΟΥ ("this place of torment") names his location explicitly.

23. **16:29 — "Moses and the Prophets" (Mōusēn kai tous prophētas):** Sinaiticus reads ΜΩΥΣΗΝ ΚΑΙ ΤΟΥΣ ΠΡΟΦΗΤΑΣ. Abraham's reply identifies the existing Scriptures as sufficient witness. The designation ΜΩΥΣΗΣ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΠΡΟΦΗΤΑΙ echoes verse 16 (Ο ΝΟΜΟΣ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΠΡΟΦΗΤΑΙ, "the law and the prophets"), binding the parable to the preceding discourse. The imperative ΑΚΟΥΣΑΤΩΣΑΝ ΑΥΤΩΝ ("let them hear them") uses the third-person imperative — a command mediated through Abraham rather than addressed directly. The verb ΑΚΟΥΩ here carries its full Hebraic weight: not merely "hear" but "hear and obey" (cf. the Shema, Deut 6:4).

24. **16:30 — "if someone from the dead should go to them" (ean tis apo nekrōn poreuthē pros autous):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΑΝ ΤΙΣ ΑΠΟ ΝΕΚΡΩΝ ΠΟΡΕΥΘΗ ΠΡΟΣ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ. The rich man's ΟΥΧΙ ΠΑΤΕΡ ΑΒΡΑΑΜ ("No, father Abraham") is a direct contradiction of the patriarch — remarkable audacity, even in torment. His counter-proposal assumes that a supernatural visitation (ΤΙΣ ΑΠΟ ΝΕΚΡΩΝ, "someone from among the dead") would succeed where Scripture has failed. The verb ΜΕΤΑΝΟΗΣΟΥΣΙΝ ("they will repent") reveals what he now knows was needed all along — ΜΕΤΑΝΟΙΑ ("repentance"), the reorientation of life that he himself never undertook.

25. **16:31 — "neither will they be persuaded if someone should rise from the dead" (oud' ean tis ek nekrōn anastē peisthēsontai):** Sinaiticus reads ΟΥΔ ΕΑΝ ΤΙΣ ΕΚ ΝΕΚΡΩΝ ΑΝΑΣΤΗ ΠΕΙΣΘΗΣΟΝΤΑΙ. Abraham's final declaration shifts from the rich man's ΠΟΡΕΥΘΗ ("should go") to ΑΝΑΣΤΗ ("should rise") — the verb of resurrection (ΑΝΙΣΤΗΜΙ). For Luke's readers, the irony is devastating: one named Lazarus will indeed be raised (John 11), and Jesus himself will rise from the dead, yet many will remain unpersuaded. The verb ΠΕΙΣΘΗΣΟΝΤΑΙ ("they will be persuaded") is future passive — they will not allow themselves to be convinced. The parable ends with the finality of this pronouncement: the Scriptures are sufficient; if they are refused, no miracle will avail.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence in the dual parables:** Luke 16 contains two parables linked by the theme of wealth and its proper use. The Parable of the Dishonest Steward (vv. 1-13) and the Parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus (vv. 19-31) are joined by a transitional section (vv. 14-18) in which the Pharisees' love of money provokes teachings on the law's endurance, the kingdom's arrival, and marriage. The formal translation preserves the structural connectives (ΚΑΙ, ΔΕ, ΟΥΝ) that bind these units together, resisting the temptation to smooth transitions that Luke deliberately left rough.

2. **The steward's shrewdness versus the rich man's blindness:** The verbal and thematic parallels between the two parables are preserved in translation. The steward uses wealth to secure future hospitality (v. 9); the rich man fails to use wealth to secure eternal life. The steward acts with ΦΡΟΝΙΜΩΣ ("shrewdly"); the rich man acts with no foresight at all. Both parables concern ΟΙΚΟΝΟΜΙΑ ("stewardship") — the management of what belongs to another. The formal translation maintains the Greek vocabulary connections (ΜΑΜΩΝΑΣ in vv. 9, 11, 13; ΠΙΣΤΟΣ/ΑΔΙΚΟΣ in vv. 10-12) to make these structural echoes visible.

3. **The Lazarus parable's reversals:** The formal translation preserves the precise vocabulary of reversal: ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΕΙΤΑΙ ("is comforted") answers Lazarus's earthly suffering, while ΟΔΥΝΑΣΑΙ ("you are in anguish") answers the rich man's earthly pleasure. The name Lazarus (ΛΑΖΑΡΟΣ, "God helps") is the only proper name in any of Jesus's parables — a detail that underscores the individual dignity of the poor man whom the rich man treated as invisible.

4. **Sinaiticus readings:** Sinaiticus reads ΟΙΚΕΤΗΣ ("household servant") at 16:13, where the parallel in Matthew 6:24 lacks this word. The inclusion intensifies the domestic metaphor: a servant who lives within the household cannot possibly divide his allegiance between two masters under one roof. At 16:19, Sinaiticus lacks the name "Dives" (a Latin tradition) and presents the rich man as anonymous — his namelessness contrasts sharply with Lazarus's name, suggesting that wealth without mercy erases identity before God. At 16:21, Sinaiticus reads ΑΛΛΑ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΚΥΝΕΣ ("but even the dogs"), emphasizing the dogs' licking as an additional indignity rather than a consolation.

5. **Bridging 15:1-16:31:** The formal translation preserves the continuity between chapters 15 and 16. The audience shifts from disciples (16:1) back to Pharisees (16:14), but the overarching theme remains constant: the proper response to the lost, the poor, and the outcast. Abraham's address ΤΕΚΝΟΝ ("child") to the rich man in 16:25 echoes the father's ΤΕΚΝΟΝ to the elder son in 15:31 — both are children of the household who failed to extend compassion. The formal translation maintains this verbal echo without annotation, trusting the reader to perceive the connection.
